items.title: Child-Pugh: criteria and scoring
items.contentHtml:
Child-Pugh Classification

  • Evaluates bilirubin, albumin, prothrombin time (INR), ascites and hepatic encephalopathy; each parameter scores 1-3.
  • Total score: Child A (5-6 points), Child B (7-9 points), Child C (10-15 points).
  • Prognostic and surgical utility: Child A good surgical risk; Child C relative contraindication for liver resection.
  • Trap: ascites and encephalopathy are graded clinically (absent/mild/moderate or grade 1-2 vs 3-4).

items.title: Portal hypertension: management of varicose veins
items.contentHtml:
Portal hypertension and esophageal varices

  • Portal hypertension defined by hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) >5 mmHg; clinically significant when HVPG >10 mmHg.
  • Esophageal varices: screening by endoscopy upon diagnosis of cirrhosis; if Child B/C or decompensation, repeat every 1-2 years.
  • Primary prophylaxis: propranolol or carvedilol (dose adjusted to HR 55-60 bpm) or endoscopic ligation if contraindication or large varicose veins (grade II/III).
  • Acute hemorrhage: terlipressin + ceftriaxone 1 g/day + endoscopic ligation.

items.title: Diagnostic paracentesis: analysis of ascites
items.contentHtml:
Ascites: diagnosis by paracentesis

  • Indication: de novo ascites or acute decompensation. serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAG) is measured = serum albumin − ascites albumin.
  • SAG >1.1 g/dL indicates portal hypertension (cirrhosis, heart failure); GASA <1.1 g/dL suggests peritoneal cause (tuberculosis, carcinomatosis).
  • Cell count: polymorphonuclear cells (PMN) ≥250 cells/μL defines spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
  • Trap: in hemorrhagic ascites (red blood cells >10,000/μL) subtract 1 PMN per 250 red blood cells to correct.

items.title: Hepatic encephalopathy: triggers and management
items.contentHtml:
Hepatic encephalopathy and triggering factors

  • Common factors: infection, gastrointestinal bleeding, constipation, diuretics (hypokalemia/alkalosis), sedatives (benzodiazepines), portosystemic shunt, diet hyperprotein.
  • Acute treatment: lactulose orally or in enema (dose up to 3 stools/day) + rifaximin 550 mg every 12 hours as second line or prevention of recurrence.
  • Clinical grade: West Haven (0 to 4); grade 1-2 treatable outpatient; grade 3-4 requires hospitalization.
  • Trap: rule out subdural hematoma, meningitis or sepsis before attributing to encephalopathy.

items.title: Hepatorenal syndrome: criteria and treatment
items.contentHtml:
Hepatorenal syndrome

  • Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS): functional renal failure in advanced cirrhosis with ascites; criteria: serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL, no improvement after 2 days of stopping diuretics and expansion with albumin, without proteinuria or active sediment.
  • Two types: SHR type 1 (rapid, doubling of creatinine in <2 weeks) and SHR type 2 (slow progression, refractory to diuretics).
  • First-line treatment: terlipressin (0.5-1 mg/4-6 h) + albumin (20-40 g/day). Alternative: norepinephrine + albumin.
  • Definitive: liver transplant.

items.title: EBP: diagnosis and management
items.contentHtml:
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis

  • Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP): infection of the ascitic fluid without intra-abdominal focus; diagnosis with PMN ≥250 cells/μL in paracentesis.
  • Most frequent germs: E. coli, Klebsiella, pneumococcus.
  • Empirical treatment: cefotaxime 2 g/8 h or ceftriaxone 2 g/day; duration 5-7 days.
  • Primary prophylaxis: in cirrhosis with ascites and total proteins in ascites <1.5 g/dL + Child B/C or history of SBP: norfloxacin 400 mg/day or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
  • Trap: always rule out appendicitis/cholecystitis (CT if doubts).

items.title: HCC: screening and diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Hepatocellular carcinoma and screening

  • Screening in cirrhosis or chronic hepatitis B (without cirrhosis if male >40 years or family history): abdominal ultrasound every 6 months ± alpha-fetoprotein (AFP).
  • Non-invasive diagnosis: nodule >1 cm on ultrasound with arterial enhancement pattern + lavage venous in CT/MRI (LI-RADS criteria).
  • AFP >200 ng/mL in cirrhotic patient suggests HCC, but is not diagnostic in isolation.
  • Staging: BCLC (Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer) treatment guide: resection/ablation (stage 0-A), chemoembolization (B), sorafenib/lenvatinib (C).

items.foco: Acute alcoholic hepatitis and Maddrey score
items.title: Alcoholic hepatitis: Maddrey score
items.contentHtml:
Acute alcoholic hepatitis and Maddrey score

  • Maddrey score (DF) = 4.6 × (patient TP − control TP) + serum bilirubin (mg/dL). DF ≥32 indicates severe alcoholic hepatitis with high mortality (30-50% at 28 days).
  • Treatment if DF ≥32 or encephalopathy: prednisolone 40 mg/day orally for 28 days (or pentoxifylline 400 mg/8 h if contraindication to corticosteroids, although current evidence controversial).
  • Evaluate response after 7 days with Lille score; <0.45 = responder, >0.45 = non-responder (discontinue corticosteroids).
  • Trap: rule out active infection before starting corticosteroids.

items.title: Coagulopathy: management and testing
items.contentHtml:
Coagulopathy in cirrhosis

  • Deficiency of procoagulant factors (II, V, VII, IX, X) and anticoagulants (protein C, S, antithrombin); the INR does not reflect the true prothrombotic state.
  • Do not routinely correct INR before low-risk procedures (paracentesis, endoscopy). For major procedures, consider fresh frozen plasma or prothrombin complex if INR >2.5.
  • Trap: cirrhosis is a state of rebalanced coagulopathy; the thrombotic risk (DVT, pulmonary embolism) is greater than the hemorrhagic risk in stable patients.
  • Anticoagulation: low molecular weight heparins are safe if platelets >50,000/μL.

items.title: Hypersplenism: thrombocytopenia and management
items.contentHtml:
Hypersplenism and thrombocytopenia

  • Hypersplenism: splenic sequestration of platelets and leukocytes due to portal hypertension; It causes thrombocytopenia (usually >50,000/μL) and leukopenia.
  • No specific treatment is required if platelets >20,000-30,000/μL and without bleeding. For procedures: thrombopoietin agonists (avatrombopag 60 mg/day for 5 days or lusutrombopag) approved before invasive procedures.
  • Splenectomy or transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) reserved for cases refractory.
  • Trap: rule out immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) if platelets <20,000/μL or insufficient response to agonists.

items.title: Acute decompensation: causes and management
items.contentHtml:
Causes of acute decompensation

  • Main: infection (SBP, pneumonia, UTI), digestive bleeding due to varicose veins, portal thrombosis, alcoholic hepatitis, drugs (NSAIDs, sedatives), surgery, dehydration (excessive diuretics, vomiting).
  • Evaluate: paracentesis, blood cultures, endoscopy, Doppler ultrasound (portal thrombosis).
  • Management: treat specific cause, albumin support (if paracentesis >5 L), avoid nephrotoxics.
  • Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF): acute decompensation with organ failure (renal, respiratory, circulatory); defines prognosis according to CLIF-C score.

items.title: MELD: scoring and prioritization
items.contentHtml:
Liver transplant: MELD criteria

  • MELD (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease) = 9.57 × ln(Creatinine mg/dL) + 3.78 × ln(Bilirubin mg/dL) + 11.2 × ln(INR) + 6.43; score from 6 to 40.
  • Used to prioritize liver transplant: higher score = higher mortality at 3 months without transplant.
  • MELD-Na incorporates serum sodium (improves prediction of mortality on the waiting list).
  • Indications: decompensated cirrhosis (refractory ascites, recurrent SBP, recurrent encephalopathy, HRS, HCC within Milan criteria), liver failure acute.
  • Trap: MELD >15 is typical threshold to consider transplant; Transplantation is contraindicated in active diseases (uncontrolled infection, extrahepatic cancer, active alcohol abuse).
